data.title: Carillon Reams Unconstrained Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Government shutdown creates fog

As the fourth quarter began, the government shutdown disrupted the release of key economic data, creating challenges for both investors and the U.S. Federal Reserve. This lack of visibility contributed to the “fog” referenced by Fed Chair Jerome Powell during his post-meeting press conference in October. Powell likened the Fed’s approach to that of a cautious driver slowing down in foggy conditions, emphasizing the need for prudence amid heightened uncertainty.

Fog lingers even after shutdown ends

Although the shutdown stalemate was resolved, the resulting data gap persisted through the end of the year as collection and reporting processes remained disrupted. The absence of data collection led to the omission of the October Consumer Price Index (CPI) from the series. Key components, such as owners’ equivalent rent, were held flat, artificially dampening inflation readings once November data was published. Labor market data was similarly affected as the lack of October figures eliminated monthly comparisons, and November employment numbers were delayed until after the Federal Reserve’s December meeting. Consequently, policymakers were forced to make decisions without access to a complete data set.

Federal Reserve cuts twice, outlook moderates

Chair Powell initially struck a cautious tone after the October meeting regarding further interest rate cuts. Labor data was notably uncertain as the last reliable report from September indicated that overall payrolls exceeded expectations, while private payrolls declined sharply and the unemployment rate edged up to 4.4%. His view was later countered when several influential Fed governors adopted a more dovish stance in November, leading to a 25-basis point (BP) rate cut at the December meeting. A modest uptick in equity market volatility in mid-November, driven by investor concerns over the scale of ongoing artificial intelligence (AI)-related capital expenditures, reinforced this dovish sentiment. Looking ahead, the pace of rate cuts is expected to moderate, with Fed voter opinion less uniform (more dissents during votes and variance in dot plot analysis) and markets pricing in only two cuts for calendar year 2026.

Judicial review of tariffs not yet resolved

In early November, investor attention turned to oral arguments before the U.S. Supreme Court regarding whether the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) grants the president the authority to impose tariffs without congressional approval. While no ruling was issued by quarter-end, market participants closely analyzed the arguments for indications of the court’s leanings. Consensus expectations point toward some curtailment of executive authority; however, uncertainty will persist until a final decision is rendered.

Spread sectors were mostly positive during the fourth quarter

Excess returns from mortgage-backed securities (MBS) led the way in the fourth quarter, finishing the year strong after lagging in the first half. Commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) and asset-backed securities (ABS) both had positive excess returns. In credit, high yield finished in positive territory, but investment grade (IG) credit was modestly negative after BBB-rated securities underperformed. The yield curve steepened again in the fourth quarter as the two Fed rate cuts pushed the front end of the curve lower, while the long end rose, indicating some wariness of higher inflation over time. The curve is now inverted out to the 1-year point but normalizes after.

Portfolio Review

Rate volatility continued to decline in the fourth quarter, reaching its lowest levels since 2021 near year-end, despite lingering uncertainty around data related to the government shutdown. Three consecutive 25-bp rate cuts by the Fed at the end of 2025 pushed short-term yields lower, bringing down the front end of the curve. Notably, the yield curve steepened as long-term rates remained elevated, normalizing the term structure. Despite the Fed's dovish stance late in the year, longer-term inflation expectations remained largely in check. The Fund's absolute duration positioning benefited performance during the quarter, as it had throughout 2025.

Treasury exposure was the largest positive contributor during the quarter as carry remained positive. Allocations to MBS, both agency and non-agency, were also large contributors as the asset class saw positive carry and continued spread compression throughout the quarter. The focus on the belly of the coupon curve saw positive selection effect as this portion of the curve continued its strong run of performance, generating the highest levels of excess return in the asset class. The allocation to both investment grade and high yield corporate securities saw positive performance as spreads continued tightened throughout the quarter despite being near historical levels, ending the year at close to where it began 2025 despite second-quarter volatility. ABS exposure saw continued positive performance as the team remains focused on high-quality, income-producing securities in the short duration portion of the curve. Structured macro trades provided a tailwind during the quarter. Limited exposure to CMBS was a marginal benefit during the quarter. Emerging Markets and foreign exchange were the only absolute detractors during the quarter but were positive contributors for the year.

The allocation to agency MBS grew during the quarter as spreads widened following the significant tightening in September.

Non-agency mortgages also increased during the quarter, with the team noting a widening gap in the spread between non-agency and agency securities. The allocation to IG corporates decreased slightly, as did the high yield allocation. Spreads across the corporate spectrum declined during the quarter and the team took profits and allocated elsewhere as opportunities arose. ABS exposure declined quarter over quarter following the large increase noted in the third quarter but remained an area of attractive opportunity. The weight in CMBS remained flat and continued to reside at a low absolute exposure as this asset class continues to require selectivity with limited issuance that meets the high bar for consideration. The exposure to non-U.S. dollar bonds decreased and the allocation to non-U.S. dollar currencies remained largely flat, ending the quarter with a small net non-U.S. exposure. The weight in U.S. Treasuries decreased during the quarter.

Portfolio duration marginally increased during the quarter as the yield curve continued to steepen. Duration remained slightly above neutral range, reflecting real rates that are attractive on a long-term basis but acknowledging the possible contribution to volatility of rates. Yield curve exposure was distributed across the curve with a focus on the 5- to 7-year and 7- to 10-year segments of the curve.

Outlook

Supreme Court tariff ruling likely to add uncertainty

Following the Supreme Court's oral arguments, consensus expectations suggested the justices were inclined to limit the Trump administration's authority to impose tariffs, affirming the appellate court's ruling that deemed the action unlawful. Beyond the overall judgment, the details of the decision are likely to drive near-term market reactions. For instance, if the administration is required to refund previously collected tariffs or faces significant restrictions on imposing new ones, this could stimulate additional economic activity. However, the fiscal implications would be considerable, as tariff revenues are already allocated to fund recently enacted spending. Moreover, rebating prior tariffs could present substantial administrative challenges. As a result, this issue remains a key source of uncertainty as the new year begins.

Will the AI hyperscaler boom continue?

Risk markets took a bit of a pause in November as concerns mounted over the substantial debt financing required to build large-scale data centers to support growing AI storage demands. While AI has the potential to represent a transformative paradigm shift, the sustainability of current growth levels will only be confirmed in hindsight. In the near term, this investment surge may help fuel broader economic expansion. However, if the spending cycle falters, it could create significant headwinds for numerous ancillary industries.

The Fed may slow the pace of rate cuts

The Fed cut rates in each of its last three meetings of 2025. As 2026 begins, that pace of cuts is expected to slow, with the market anticipating only two cuts for all of 2026. The dot plots indicate the Fed has become much more divided. While the current committee is clearly divided, in a few months President Trump is expected to nominate a new chair who may be more sympathetic to the lower rates desired by the administration. Additional clarity from data releases following the government shutdown should provide a better near-term picture of the path of inflation and the health of the labor market, but as always, this provides only a dim view into the future.

'Everything is going to be OK' is still a crowded trade

Current market valuations provide limited compensation for what we view as significant risks embedded in an overly optimistic narrative. Investor sentiment remains elevated despite a growing list of concerns. In this environment, a prudent approach involves minimizing the cost of maintaining a defensive posture while patiently awaiting an inevitable correction, rather than aggressively pursuing higher-risk assets that offer only modest upside if the thesis proves correct.

Please consider the investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses of any fund carefully before investing. Mortgage- and Asset-Backed Securities are subject to prepayment risk and the risk of default on the underlying mortgages or other assets.

High-yield securities involve greater risk than investment grade securities and tend to be more sensitive to economic conditions and credit risk.

Derivatives such as options, futures contracts, currency forwards or swap agreements may involve greater risks than if the Fund invested in the referenced obligation directly. Derivatives are subject to risks such as market risk, liquidity risk, interest rate risk, credit risk and management risk. Derivative investments could lose more than the principal amount invested. These losses could exceed the amount originally invested.

The Fund may, at times, experience higher than average portfolio turnover, which may generate significant taxable gains and increased trading expenses, which, in turn, may lower the Fund's return.

Short-sale risk includes the potential loss of more money than the actual cost of the investment, and the risk that the third party to the short sale may fail to honor its contract terms, causing a loss to the fund.

Bond Ratings: Ratings are by Moody's, S&P, and/or Fitch. Ratings provided by nationally recognized statistical rating organizations, also called ratings agencies, are appraisals of a particular issuer's creditworthiness, including the possibility that the issuer will not be able to pay interest or repay principal. Ratings are not recommendations to buy, sell or hold a security, nor do ratings remove market risk. Securities with the same rating can actually trade at significantly different prices. In addition, ratings are subject to review, revision, suspension, reduction or withdrawal at any time, and a rating agency may place an issuer under review or credit watch. Additionally, Fitch reports are available for municipal bonds. More about ratings is available at Moody's - credit ratings, research, and data for global capital markets , S&P Global Ratings , and Fitch Ratings: Credit Ratings & Analysis For Financial Markets .

Definitions

Allocation effect – A measure of the return generated by allocating portfolio weights to particular sectors, industries, segments, or geographies.

Artificial intelligence (AI) – A technology that enables computers and machines to simulate human learning, comprehension, problem solving, decision making, creativity, and autonomy.

Basis points (BPs) – Measurements used in discussions of interest rates and finance. One basis point equals 1/100th of 1%, or 0.01%.

BBB-rated securities – Securities at the lower end of the investment grade rating spectrum.

Capital expenditures / capex – Monies used by a company to buy, improve, or maintain physical assets such as real estate, facilities, technology, or equipment, and may include new projects or investments.

Carry – The net return or cost of holding a bond over a specified period of time, based on the difference in the income it generates and the cost of financing the holding.

Consensus estimates – Forecasts of a public company's projected earnings, the results of a particular industry, sector, geography, asset class, or other category, or the expected findings of a macroeconomic report based on the combined estimates of analysts and other market observers who track the data in question.

Consumer Price Index (CPI) – Measures the change in prices paid by consumers for goods and services. The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics bases the index on prices of food, clothing, shelter, transportation, doctors' and dentists' services, drugs, and other goods and services that people buy for day-to-day living. Prices are collected each month in 75 urban areas across the country from about 6,000 households and 22,000 retailers.

Correction – A decline in the market price of a security or index of more than 10% from its recent highs but not more than 20%.

Coupon curve – A line that represents the relationship between coupon rates of similar bonds – for example, those with issuers or credit ratings in common – and their time to maturity.

Credit spread – The difference in yield between a U.S. Treasury bond and another debt security with the same maturity but different credit quality. Also referred to as “bond spreads” or “default spreads,” credit spreads are measured in basis points, with a 1% difference in yield equaling a spread of 100 basis points. Credit spreads reflect the debt security's risk compared with that of Treasury bond – which is considered to be risk-free – and they are considered to be “tighter” when the spread is smaller or “looser” when the spread is larger.

Credit spread compression – A narrowing of credit spreads, often happening when strong demand brings down the yield on a higher-yielding bond.

Credit spread tightening / widening – The contraction (tightening) or expansion (widening) of credit spreads in response to changes in economic conditions that cause credit risk to decline (causing tightening) or increase (causing widening).

Defensive – Investment strategies characterized by rebalancing the investment portfolio regularly to maintain an intended asset allocation. They typically entail investing in high-quality, short-maturity bonds and blue-chip stocks, diversifying across sectors and countries, and holding cash and cash equivalents in down markets.

Dot plot – A U.S. Federal Reserve chart summarizing the Federal Open Market Committee's (FOMC) outlook for the federal funds rate. Each dot represents the interest rate forecasted by one of the 12 members of the committee.

Dovish, hawkish and centrist – Characterizations of the monetary policy preferences of central bankers and others. Doves tend to support maintaining lower interest rates, often in support of stimulating job growth and the economy more generally. Hawks prioritize controlling inflation and may favor raising interest rates to reduce it or keep it in check. Centrists tend to occupy the middle of the continuum between tight (hawkish) and loose (dovish) monetary policy.

Duration – A measure that incorporates a bond's yield, coupon, final maturity and call features into one number, expressed in years, that indicates how price-sensitive a bond or portfolio is to changes in interest rates. Bonds with higher durations carry more risk and have higher price volatility than bonds with lower durations.

Fiscal policy – The tax collection and spending a government uses to influence its country's economy.

Foreign exchange, forex, FX – The trading of one currency for another.

Headwind – An event or market force that hinders the prospects for performance in an individual investment or group of investments.

High yield – Bonds with credit ratings below BBB- from Standard & Poor's or below Baa3 from Moody's.

Hyperscalers – The largest cloud computing providers that can provide massive amounts of computing resources and storage at enterprise scale.

International Emergency Economic Powers Act / IEEPA – A U.S. law that provides the president broad authority to regulate a variety of economic transactions following a declaration of national emergency. In 2025, the act was used to impose or raise U.S. tariffs on imports from many countries.

Investment grade – Fixed-income securities rated BBB or better by Standard & Poor's or Baa or better by Moody's.

Monetary policy – The decisions made by central banks to raise or lower benchmark interest rates or otherwise tighten or loosen credit to influence an economy's growth, inflation, or employment levels.

Overweight – describes a portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is greater than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Owners' equivalent of rent of primary residences / OER – A component of the Consumer Price Index that helps measure changes in the cost of shelter in the United States. It is based on the answers from consumers who own their homes to the question: "If someone were to rent your home today, how much do you think it would rent for monthly, unfurnished and without utilities?"

Payroll report, officially known as the Employment Situation Summary – A monthly U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) report tracking nonfarm payroll employment and the national unemployment rate, with data on changes in average hourly earnings, and job trends in public and private sectors of employment. The report is based on surveys of households and employers.

Positioning – Assessments of whether professional investors are, on the whole, bullish or bearish on a particular security, industry, sector, market capitalization or other area of the market, as reflected by the extent to which they are invested in the area of the market in question.

Real interest rate – is an interest rate that has been adjusted to remove the effects of inflation. Once adjusted, it reflects the real cost of funds to a borrower and the real yield to a lender or to an investor.

Securitized products – Products, such as asset-backed securities (ABS) and mortgage-backed securities (MBS), that are created by pooling loans from a variety of sources and issuing bonds that are backed by these loans. Creditworthiness stems from the credit quality of the underlying loans, as opposed to corporate bonds in which creditworthiness is derived from the earning power of the issuing company.

Spread sector – Nongovernmental fixed-income securities, such as corporate bonds or mortgage bonds, whether investment grade or high yield, that provide an additional yield, or "spread," over the yield of a risk-free government bond.

Tailwind – is a term used to describe events or market forces that exert a positive influence on an investment's performance.

Underweight – A portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is less than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Valuation – The current or projected worth of an investment. Absolute valuations reflect the absolute value of an investment considered singly, and relative valuations consider the value of an investment against comparable investments.

Visibility – The degree to which market observers and participants can reliably estimate the direction and extent of future performance or other trends in securities, sectors, asset classes, markets, or economies.

Yield curve – A line that plots yields (interest rates) of bonds having equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. The slope of the yield curve gives an idea of future interest rate changes and economic activity. A steepening yield curve results from a widening in the difference between short- and long-term interest rates. Investors and market analysts watch certain yield curves for signs of inversion, when yields for longer-term debt instruments fall below yields on short-term debt with the same credit quality. The front or short end of the yield curve refers to groups of bonds with the shortest maturity dates. The belly of the curve refers to bonds that mature generally in 2 to 10 years. The long end of the curve refers to bonds with maturities of 10 years or more.

Benchmark Index

The ICE BofA US 3-Month Treasury Index measures the performance of a single issue of outstanding treasury bill which matures closest to, but not beyond, three months from the rebalancing date. The issue is purchased at the beginning of the month and held for a full month; at the end of the month that issue is sold and rolled into a newly selected issue.

data.title: Waste Management: A Top Defensive Stock For A Bumpy 2026
data.description:
In late November 2025, I reiterated my buy rating on Waste Management, Inc. (WM) and expressed my confidence regarding the company's fundamentals and outlook. So far, that update has aged relatively well, with the stock up around 8% since then. The company reported their latest earnings quite recently but missed on both the top and bottom lines. That might be a surprise for a company that has demonstrated stability and resilience for decades. Today, I will analyze their results and see if there is anything to worry about for shareholders.

Below, it is shown that Waste Management's top line growth rate has suffered, likely as a result of an unfavorable environment. However, Q4 was still a resilient quarter as margin improvements came to the rescue. Furthermore, cash flows looked solid, and heading into 2026, capital returns are set to increase. As for their guidance, the coming fiscal year is expected to look quite a bit like 2025 Q4, but that's not necessarily a bad thing. With the valuation being quite distant below the mid 2025 multiyear high, I believe long term investors are presented with an opportunity to buy or add to positions. Therefore, I'm reiterating my buy rating on Waste Management.

Revenue Growth Dives

Let's get started with a look at their top line results. For 2025 Q4, Waste Management posted revenues of $6.313 billion. While a growth rate of 7% isn't bad, you can clearly see above that there have now been two straight quarters of deceleration. Furthermore, the slowdown from Q3's 14.9% increase has been rather sharp. As stated above, the company also missed expectations, as they underperformed estimates by a considerable margin of $73.72 million. We'll have to look for answers in their guidance later in this article, but judging from these top line results, it does seem that this macro environment is not in their favor. YoY, Collection and Disposal core price dropped from 6.7% to 6.3% as a signal of headwinds. Overall, the company seems to be losing some momentum in its business.

While Waste Management doesn't like to list the growth seen in their Healthcare Solutions since the segment is still a relatively small contributor to overall revenues, there is no question that their push in this direction is starting to pay off. As you can see in the table above, Healthcare Solutions helped to accelerate the top line growth rate for the company significantly both for Q4 and the full year, with their Legacy Business showing some sluggishness. To be specific, in Q4, their Healthcare Solutions saw a 53% increase in revenues YoY. This shows that management's strategic decision to expand into the healthcare waste sector is justified.

Margins Improve & EPS Growth Picks Up

While the deceleration for revenue growth is definitely undesirable, Waste Management's EBITDA margin strength helped to offset some of that weakness. Adjusted EBITDA is one of the company's preferred measures of profitability, and I agree that this metric does a good job at summing up the company's overall business efficiency.

For the company as a whole, adjusted EBITDA margin was 31.3%, and that represents a sizeable expansion of 240 basis points YoY. This is no small expansion, and the fact that both their Legacy Business and their Healthcare Solutions saw YoY improvement makes it even more of a sign that their business is experiencing broad efficiency gains.

For their Legacy Business segment, factors that contributed to margin expansion included disciplined cost initiatives, while for their Healthcare Solutions, the integration of the segment into their overall business has led to "improved processes and synergy capture." Therefore, as a whole, there are great signs for Waste Management's profitability.

As a reflection of the margin strength, Waste Management saw solid levels of bottom line growth. For Q4, their adjusted EPS of $1.93 represented YoY growth of 14%. This is quite a nice acceleration from just a 1% increase seen back in Q3. Even though the company still missed expectations by $0.02, I would say the fact that they were able to accelerate the bottom line while top line growth slowed is an impressive feat of business efficiency. With the macro environment not being the most favorable, it seems Waste Management has put a significant focus on improving the internals of the business. Investors should be encouraged by this, as it means they are positioning the business well for the long term.

Cash Flows & Returns

Overall, Waste Management's cash flows are in good shape, and that reflects a healthy business. In Q4, they had operating cash flows worth $1.698 billion, and you can see above that this represents a respectable growth rate of 12%. This shows that cash generation is strong. There was a large difference between operating cash flow growth and FCF growth, but that was because CapEx moderated significantly. Nonetheless, shareholders seeing FCF growth at multiyear highs should be happy.

While the company didn't do any stock buybacks in 2025 and their dividend yield isn't the highest, there are some signs that the company is willing to share these strong cash flows with shareholders. In mid-December, it was announced that the company would increase the 2026 dividend by 14.5%, and so investors looking for income may be attracted by that. Furthermore, a $3 billion share repurchase authorization was approved, and so moving forward, there will likely be considerably more capital returns than in 2025. Therefore, the strong cash flows seem to be making a real difference for shareholders.

Full Year Outlook

Let's now take a look at the numbers Waste Management expects for the full year 2026. For revenues, they see a range of $26.425 billion to $26.625 billion. This is midpoint growth of 5.2%. This is quite a drastic drop from 14.2% in 2025, and so it seems that weak business momentum will continue due to an unfavorable environment.

For adjusted EBITDA margin, they are projecting 30.8% to 31.0%. The midpoint presents a 30 basis point expansion. For 2025 as a whole, there was a 40 basis expansion, but nonetheless it seems that margin strength will carry on into 2026. The company is, therefore, once again counting on business efficiency improvements to power profitability.

Lastly, for FCF, they believe the figure should be in the range of $3.75 billion and $3.85 billion. A midpoint growth rate of 29.4% for 2026 would outpace the 2025 increase of 26.8%, and so shareholders need not worry about the company's capital returns. As a whole, I would say 2026 is set to look quite a bit like 2025's Q4. Not great revenue results, but margins and cash flows will likely step in to save the day.

Valuation Analysis

Waste Management's forward P/E ratio has bounced back from January lows, but at a current reading of 28.55, it remains well off the multiyear high seen near mid-2025. I believe the stock remains attractive based on where the valuation is.

In today's analysis, it was shown that the company is not operating in a particularly favorable environment. The slowdown in revenue growth shows that clearly. However, the company has managed to make significant improvements internally to boost profitability. As discussed earlier, FCFs and capital returns are looking favorable for shareholders, and full year 2026 guidance shows that this will continue.

While I believe that the forward P/E ratio is justifiably below its 2025 high, I also find the valuation a bit low relative to what we have seen in recent years, as the company is being increasingly well run by its management team. The fundamentals are solid, and what the company really needs is just a change in fortunes with the macro environment. Therefore, long term investors should take advantage of the current valuation.

Concluding Remarks

Given how 2025 ended and how 2026 has started for the market, defense may be the best offense right now for investors. I believe at current levels, a great choice could be Waste Management stock. The company has demonstrated resilience and stability time and time again, and with management focused on the internals of the business, this resilience will only get stronger in my view. Furthermore, the growing dividend and new buyback authorization are great rewards to help shareholders sit through the company's own bout of top line sluggishness, and so I find Waste Management to be quite attractive at the current valuation. As a result, I've decided to maintain my buy rating on the stock.

data.title: XP Inc.: Shifting Its Advisory Strategy, And Trades At Attractive Earnings Multiple
data.description:
XP Inc. (XP) released 4Q25 results, showing a general continuation of the trends we have been commenting on in previous articles: relative stagnation in new clients and net new money, but good performance in asset growth thanks to high-interest rates.

A significant move regarding the qualitative concerns I raised in previous articles is the recognition of service and reputational challenges with the advisor model (which I have long considered problematic). The company shared a new strategy that will govern the advisors more strictly and offer clients the possibility of using a fee-based model.

Although the price has moved up around 12% since I last wrote about XP, I continue to believe the name is attractively priced. Even assuming a depreciation of the BRL, the name trades at 12x TTM earnings, with the possibility to grow under the current model, but also to have a cyclical shift towards the much more profitable equity-heavy allocation regime, if rates go down in Brazil. I maintain a Buy rating.

4Q25 results

The company's results were not particularly surprising for anyone following XP throughout the past two or three years.

We maintain a slow gain of clients (2% YoY), with a stagnant but still interesting R$20 billion addition of net new retail client money (R$32 billion when considering corporate clients). This represents organic, ex-financial growth in client assets (of ~R$1.5 trillion) of around 8%.

In addition to the net new client money, the company's client assets (the ultimate drivers of profitability) are impacted by the high-interest rates in the country (15%). If I have a simple interest rate product with XP, even if I don't invest any new money, as long as I don't redeem money, the assets will grow at 15%. The combination of net new money and the appreciation under several categories of assets (equities or derivatives do not benefit from this) led to total client assets + AUM + AUA to grow 22% YoY. Client assets, a more restrictive consideration, grew 16%.

Net revenues, the next line in the results, grew 10%. We can notice here some challenges in terms of margins or take rate, as seen below.

Margins were broadly stable, with EBT also up 10% for the year. For the quarter, EBT was up 20%, with margins up 250, but this was driven by a particularly good quarter in corporate & issuer (investment banking) services, which is a little more lumpy.

SG&A was up 10% for the quarter and 8% for the year, driven partly by people (headcount up 9%, people expenses up 5%), and non-people (mainly technology investments), up 13%. XP's business should show better operational leverage, but the company has said that it is investing in its service levels (more on this below).

In terms of sources and allocations, things did not change much. Equities presented a positive YoY quarter (+3%), something that had not happened in a long time, but were down for the year (-5%). Fixed income was up 12% (again, in line with national interest rates), and other services gained share (retirement plans, credit cards, insurance), in the aggregate up 16% for the year. This trend has already been analyzed, as XP moves from a broker to a more 'bank'-like 'offering to its clients, with a focus on financial services more than loans.

Also, as commented in previous quarters, the investment banking side of the company (Corporate & Issuer Services) is also gaining share, up 20% for the full year (and 50% for the year in the quarter, as mentioned). The big driver of this movement is XP's capacity to collocate debt among retail clients.

Changes in the advisory model

Since I started covering XP, I have been a critic of the company's advisory model, which was mostly based on transaction fees. This obviously creates a conflict of interest between the advisor and the client, at least in the short term. The potential risk from this was reputational damage to the company if advisors recommended bad products.

Since 2Q25, XP has been revising its advisory model, and during this quarter's call, the company provided more comments about strengthening governance (that is, controlling more), for example, via technology and KPI monitoring of client portfolios, while offering clients the possibility to choose a fee-based model instead of a commission/transaction-based model (25%+ of AUA is already under this modality). The company also provided data on advised portfolio performance (citing, for example, that 90% of portfolios did better than 90% of the national interest rate).

This all indicates an understanding of the risk the transaction-advisory model implies, and I believe it is a healthy development. Aligned advisor-client interest ends up driving higher balances and higher revenues down the road, while protecting the brand.

Valuation remains attractive

XP generated EPS of R$9.8 for the year and R$2.5 in the quarter. Both come to about $1.85/1.9 at the current exchange rate (R$5.2). Compared to that, a stock price of $20 represents a P/E TTM of about 10.5x.

This does not seem like a lot for a company growing the top line and earnings by about 10% YoY. Furthermore, the company is well-positioned for two potential Brazilian macro scenarios.

If rates remain high, then the company will continue to grow on 'autopilot' simply because its assets under custody grow. If rates decrease, the company can benefit from higher debt issuance activity (companies will try to refinance at lower rates) and also from clients shifting towards higher revenue-generating equity products. This will partly be offset by a lower float gain as rates decrease.

A negative scenario would be rates decreasing while the stock market underperforms, which could happen either because there is a recession in Brazil or because of political volatility (the country will have elections at the end of 2026).

Finally, an additional risk is the depreciation of the BRL (the majority, if not all, of the company's earnings are denominated in BRL). If the BRL depreciated towards 6.2 per USD (the lowest point reached in 2024), then at current earnings capacity, the company would generate $1.6 per share in net income, or a P/E of 12.5x, not particularly high either.

I believe XP continues to present an attractive valuation, and therefore maintain a Buy rating.

data.title: LPL Financial: AI Selloff Is Your Opportunity
data.description:
LPL Financial (LPLA) remains the #1 independent broker-dealer in the United States, with ~32.2k financial advisors served and ~$2.4tn in assets under custody. The core LPL value proposition is simple—provide a comprehensive suite of solutions, across the front, middle, and back offices, allowing financial advisors to run their businesses independently in return for fees.

This playbook has worked well and allowed LPL to grow both ways—both by gaining market share and riding structural "demand for advice" tailwinds. Hence, its stellar rate of compounding through the cycles.

Of course, just because LPL was a compounder doesn’t mean it will remain one. Take this week, for instance, when LPL, along with its broker peers, sold off sharply on AI concerns. So much so that LPL has now retraced all of the gains made since I last covered the stock (here and here).

Now, the threat of AI disruption isn’t new (ChatGPT is over three years old); nor is the concept of automated financial planning (e.g., robo-advisors). But new products, such as the tax planning tool recently unveiled by privately held ~$1.9bn company Altruist, are hitting very close to home for LPL.

Will AI ultimately slash LPL’s terminal value and undo the gains made under previous CEO Dan Arnold and current CEO Rich Steinmeier? Time will tell. But in light of a very sharp selloff and with a few turns taken off the earnings multiple, the LPLA investment case is well worth a re-look.

Recapping the AI-Driven Brokerage Selloff

The "AI-forward" wealth platform, Altruist, recently unveiled an interesting new AI-powered tax planning tool. The claim is that this tool, available on its Hazel AI platform, will "turn client tax data into clear tax plans in minutes." That means no manual prompting or data inputs are needed; simply upload the relevant documents and customer data, and Hazel models out the scenarios and plans.

In response, LPL, along with major brokers like Charles Schwab (SCHW) and Ameriprise Financial (AMP) took sizeable intraday hits to their share prices. Not unlike the sharp selloffs we’ve seen this year in insurance and software when industry-specific AI use cases emerged.

The question, though, is whether AI disruption is really that much of a risk for LPL and its brokerage peers?

Pondering the AI Risk/Reward

1. Advisor Pricing & Efficiency

Intuitively, the biggest hit to the brokerage P&L is via pricing. After all, in a world where AI commoditizes financial advice and reduces switching costs, LPL’s advisors are likely to see their fees compress.

The silver lining, though, is that AI can also benefit the advisors. If, for instance, Altruist’s tool allows advisors to bypass the grunt work (e.g., data entry, formatting, etc.), that allows for two possibilities. First, it frees up bandwidth, allowing advisors to better focus on client outcomes. Secondly, it gives advisors the capacity to prospect and take on more clients; this also means reduced per-client costs (same cost base spread over more customers) and potentially even, a better P&L outcome.

Thus, it isn’t a given, in my view, that a proliferation of industry-specific AI tools hurts advisor profitability. If anything, a K-shaped outcome seems more likely - advisors that maximize the efficiency opportunities for AI are likely to outperform those who don’t.

2. The Platform Flywheel

The other big question is the platform-level implication: will advisors continue to need the LPL platform in an AI-disrupted world? The rising level of complexity associated with the independent model suggests the answer is a resounding "yes."

Consider that the entry barriers to an advisor going independent are only growing, from infrastructure to compliance to product access. And the fact that none of these have been comprehensively solved by AI tools, which, for now, remain limited to automating tasks, rather than entire functions.

What seems more likely to happen, in my view, is that as LPL bolts on more AI tools like Hazel onto its platform, scale economies work in its favor. And as LPL becomes an ever more attractive destination over smaller platforms, this means more assets, "stickier" assets, and more fee opportunities to come.

3. The "Human Touch"

But what if AI doesn’t actually disrupt the financial planning industry? After all, LPL, along with the broader industry, has only gone from strength to strength through prior tech advancements. The most notable of which were "robo advisors", low-cost digital investment platforms that automate portfolio management based on an underlying algorithm, which have existed for many years now.

The key here is the "human touch." In a world where AI can output tax, portfolio allocation, and estate management advice, would individual investors trust it over an in-person meeting with an advisor?

Today, the answer, in my view, is "no".

As highlighted in Lloyds Banking Group’s (LYG) 2025 consumer survey, the vast majority of consumers within the retirement age bracket do not trust AI-generated advice over "traditional sources." In the 55-64 age group, the gap was ~33 points (11% AI vs. 44% advisor); this widens to ~56 points for the >75 age group (3% AI vs. 59% advisor).

A closer look at the reasons for client preferences suggests this trust gap isn’t likely to be overcome anytime soon. Key barriers such as data privacy and security (43% very concerned), followed by inaccuracy (34%) and AI bias (26%), aren't likely to be addressed by model advancements.

The results here tally with Vanguard’s recent study highlighting that advisor clients really value "peace of mind." Specifically, this means more support ("My advice provider is watching my investments and keeping me on track"), trust ("I trust my advice provider to put my needs first"), and less worry ("I wouldn't stress about my finances as much").

In sum, even if we do get to a reality where AI advisors become as capable as their human counterparts (we are a long way from this), there will always be a personal and emotional component that AI cannot disrupt. As long as this "human touch" remains relevant, so should LPL.

Odds in Your Favor

AI disruption concerns have subtracted a substantial % of LPL’s market cap over the last week. But was the selloff warranted? My take is no, as platforms like LPL, and their advisors, still have a very important role to play amid a structurally "growing demand for advice."

In any case, the odds are in your favor. LPL’s forward P/E multiple is now a few turns lower, despite a very solid Q4 2025 result, at ~11x one-year forward earnings. This is for a company that’s grown earnings at an annualized +25% pace, maintained best-in-class retention rates, and has a lot of runway into the decades to come.

While there are risks, which, in addition to AI, include competition, interest rate movements, and post-M&A integration, management’s track record, as well as LPL’s current valuation buffer, keeps the risk/reward very favorable.

Net-net, the combination of a discounted P/E multiple (that will eventually normalize) and above-par earnings growth should underpin a very nice return for patient, long-term-oriented investors.

data.title: FMC Corporation: High-Risk Play In Fertilizers That I'm Unwilling To Enter
data.description:
Readers have contacted me over the past few months, wanting me to start coverage on the company FMC Corporation (FMC). I've never been opposed to it, but my coverage on fertilizers and agrochemicals has been at capacity for some time - though this is at least partially an excuse. The simple fact is, that I've never been a fan of the fundamental weakness that I've been able to see in FMC, even before the crash that we saw in 3Q of 2025. The recent pieces of news have not made this any easier, not in the least.

You know me for a fundamental valuation investor, if you follow my work. The importance is in those two concepts. Fundamentals, one, meaning I want strong fundamentals or at least something I can understand. Secondly, valuation. Once I understand it, I want a good price for the relative risk that I am taking. The reason I have been so reticent on FMC is because I believe that the company has either failed to deliver one of those things or both.

This article represents my first entry (and possibly last; we'll see what happens) into coverage for the company according to my valuation model, but don't mistake that for a high conviction rating. In fact, I rate it a "SELL". In this case, I believe that Seeking Alpha's valuation and Quant grades very nicely encapsulate what the picture of this company is. Quant has it at 11/12 (in spots in the industry), and 260 out of 280 in the sector. It's not a good ranking. Literally every single factor grade apart from valuation is at a D+ or worse - and the only reason that valuation is at A+ is because you're getting the companies for what in the long term could amount to "pennies on the dollar".

Many analysts have been positive on the company, especially since the drop. They see the value in the assets, and the potential for an upside that certainly is here.

But given what we're seeing in the fertilizer/agrochemical sector, is it worth it? Is it a risk worth taking here, given what the company said about its 2026E?

Let's see what we've got here.

FMC - Issues were clear 3+ years ago

The reason I have stayed out for so long was that FMC presented some of the most unappealing fundamentals in the sector - and it did not take the 2025 results or the downturn to bring that. Warning signs, to any analyst who was looking, were clear. In early 2023, the company started seeing destocking across multiple key channels. While this was in no way unique at the time, the degree, coupled with the company's market exposures to markets like Brazil, which wasn't as crucial for Yara (YARIY) or others, was worrying. Anyone who looked at top-line sales declines could see more than a 25% decline in a single year, and the fact that it was so much more than management had guided for, was one of the first signs.

The reason I like Yara, and even Bayer (if we want to compare agrochemicals more purely), is its conservative financials and fundamentals. Low debt, high cash flow, and capital mean that when, not if, a downturn hits, Yara is capable of fending this off with its reserves for quite some time before things get "bad".

That's not, dear readers, the case with FMC. In 2023, the company had a debt load of over $4B net. When you combine this with its 2024E FCF of negative $800+ or so, you could see very early on what the danger for this company was. If you're a company burning through cash during a downcycle, things can get bad - real bad. Case in point, FMC was forced to lower its prices despite low margins at the time, while others were stabilizing or even raising them.

Very little of what FMC has gone through the past 2-3 years has been good news or positive trends. The poor performance in India was just "icing on the risk cake" here. When FMC reported revenues that had not yet been sold to final customers, I was already not interested in the company, but this seemed to be the final straw to many institutional investors, who then left the investment. And it's not though, after 3-4 successive guidance cuts between 2023-2025, that investors were not warned.

Staying in an investment because you believe in a turnaround, while fundamentals stay solid, is one thing. Staying in it while fundamentals and earnings significantly worsen, which means the valuation decline has correlation, is another thing - at least to me.

So, FMC. The company is an agroscience company working in both fertilizers and various types of plant chemicals. It's not as "pureplay" as, say, Yara, and focuses more on protection, agrotech, and the like. Overall, not a bad company nor a bad sector to be in - complementary to pure-play fertilizers (FMC produces fertilizers such as Crop Up and NPK 6-23-1, as well as various plant stimulators), even. I've invested plenty in Bayer and made good profits, and I've invested plenty in Yara, same good profits (and more there). In the end, both fertilizer and agrochemical companies focus on improving crop yields.

Many of its products are sold in NA, APAC, and LATAM. Brazil, in particular, is a major revenue source for FMC due to high-yield corn and soybean production, and FMC has focused large sales efforts on it. Through the M&A of Cheminova, the company is growing in Europe/EMEA as well.

India was once a good market for the company, but unfortunately, the company is selling the segment, valued at about $450M, to reduce debt. As of 4Q25, the company's net debt to EBITDA was about 4.1x, which for this sort of company is very high. As a comparison, Yara is at 1.17x, Bayer is below 3x.

FMC reported its 4Q25 results not long ago, according to the timing of this article. It's a good coincidence, because my stance is that even in the event of the company basically hitting all of its targets and priorities, which you see above, the company would still be fundamentally questionable. The debt paydown won't bring its net debt down below 3x- and FMC is still forecasting lower prices as I am writing this article for 1Q26. Volumes may be up, but pricing is still dropping, and India remains a headwind (5% for 1Q26E), despite FX tailwinds reported.

Combining lower pricing and unfavorable manufacturing costs, the picture is not good. Furthermore, about $20M in tariff headwinds. The company's outlook for adjusted EPS is still negative here, with an estimated AEPS of negative $0.44/share.

Whenever you're looking to invest in a company with negative AEPS that has had fundamental troubles for some time, one of the first questions to ask is, "When will things turn around"? The recent dividend cut does not make it easier, and the fact that the "strategic review" is ongoing further complicates matters. It has gotten so bad that FMC is considering selling off business units.

How exactly would that work? Is it realistic, or even likely? It's obvious that $2.2B+ in net losses requires some sort of management action. So retaining services from legal and financial advisors is entirely understandable. However, the company's view that the stock price doesn't reflect its asset base - well, the debt is part of the fundamentals, thanks to company action and management action. This was further emphasized by the rating cut from Moody's in early 2026 to junk status, or Ba1.

Their mere statement that they are open to selling it means that there are PE firms currently in rooms, calculating what would be possible to "extract" from this company following a sale.

6 months ago, positive AEPS called for $5.02 in 2028E with a high yield, about $4.65 in 2027, and about $4/share in 2026E. Now the forecast is less than half that - and that's on an adjusted basis.

The thing is, PE firms have consumed FMC "parts" before. In 2013, Peroxygens was sold to a PE firm at about $200M, and Cinven bought Global Specialty Solutions in 2024 for about $350M. Therefore, the risk of the company buying the entire company is, in my view, relatively low. Rather, I would "pick and choose" the chocolate from the cookie here - it's unlikely that all of the company is attractive for purchase.

This also leads me to how I consider the company in terms of valuation.

Valuation

This is one of those rare times when I'm not giving the company a clear valuation. My experience with LBOs or PE firms is limited, but it's there.

So let me be clear on this - in most PE/LBO (leveraged buyouts), common shareholders do not keep their shares. They typically get an amount over the price before the announcement. The real danger is if not all parts of the company are sold. This would initiate a carve-out process, where sales proceeds are unlikely to land in your pockets (unless you sell on announcement), and you're instead stuck in a multi-year turnaround, which would then be dependent on what hypothetical parts of the company remain and are attractive.

A PE is not going to buy unattractive assets at appealing prices - they're likely to want the highest-margin, high-potential businesses. To get even close to $1-2B out of a deal like that, based on previous PE deals for FMC, this company would likely have to sell off part of its "identity" and core.

The notion that a buyer is going to price your assets at a price that "you want", or at a price that's appealing to you, during a fire sale/debt restructuring, is very naive. If you have FMC shares, and you've held them for long, it's my stance that you're unlikely to recoup your investment (I may be wrong, of course).

A PE firm would likely value the company depending on the debt paydown plan. But given the company's very volatile earnings, the potential analysis here is very dangerous to begin with. Existing debt makes even PE deals sensitive to interest rates and operational underperformance. Because of the market backdrop for fertilizers, which I've gone into in my ICL (ICL) article, I don't see this as likely to outperform in 2026.

Instead of putting a clear valuation from the get-go, let me pose a question back to you.

At what price would you buy a distressed, uncertain EBITDA, highly leveraged company with market issues in a volatile market that's experiencing top- and bottom-line weaknesses?

Because that needs to be the question. As a buyer, you wouldn't listen to arguments about how the company "may" be nice in the future. The 5-year EBITDA average is about 15x. If I were a PE company, I wouldn't go in above 3-5x here - but again, this would depend on a lot of moving parts. The current book value is at 0.87x. You might think this is low, but it's actually not that low. When you consider how low the company has fallen, you'd expect it to be maybe 5x lower than its peers, but it's not even half of what Bayer is trading at.

If FMC wants to sell off Crop Protection, then we need to see key segment data for that segment. If it's agroscience and R&D, then we need to see that (maybe higher multiples). The problem is that the company provides regional, not clear sector, results or forecasts. This makes valuing it harder, because the company isn't (likely) going to sell more geographies (though we'd have to see).

The high use of debt, the distressed state of the business, and the considerations that a PE company is likely to do mean that I don't view the company as investable at this point, except in the most extreme speculative scenario. If you want a good upside from that hypothetical deal price (and I stress: hypothetical), you'd need to buy at significantly below what the company would be sold, or sell its parts for - you'd also (if the company isn't sold in its entirety) need to be willing to hold for a long time, likely at sub-par returns.

To account for that, I would be unwilling to buy above 3.5x EBITDA, and above 0.4x book value. A low-end 3.5x $720M EBITDA level implies a $2.5B EV for the entire company - about $20/share based on shares outstanding. But then we also have the debt, which puts the share price technically negative. I realize this is also well below the company's targets for sales proceeds, but that's how I value it. That's the issue here. Bullish investors are citing macroeconomic and demographic trends in support of their stance - I believe we're way past that here.

I just don't believe there's a whole lot of value here. The company's legacy products are facing generic pressure, the diamides are losing margins, and the growth focus and R&D is an uncertain segment at best. Current SOTP valuations range from between $17-$38/share. Analyst targets for the company are now at a low of $13/share to a high of $40/share, with an average of $18/share - but despite the upside, only 4 out of 17 investors are at "BUY".

I would buy shares in FMC at a single-digit share price. Below $10/share, so a PT of $9.9. And even then, I would be very careful. This means reducing a significant debt impact from the EV/EBITDA, and hoping for a great deal of upside from a PE sale. It's below the lowest estimates. I use it because I don't see a recovery for its legacy or its diamide segments in the current market, and I see the downgrade in ratings further pressuring the company's financials and interest rate payments (which have been growing more than 60% in less than 4 years).

This is a very bearish thesis, but it's one I can "stand behind".

My thesis is as follows.

Thesis

I believe this company is in deep distress and very hard to profitably invest in. Its current state as an admitted PE target (potentially selling the business), means that it's extremely difficult to see where we are in a year, or even 6 months. What's clear is that it's not a good time to be a long-term FMC shareholder if you bought at a high price.

The only way I would invest in this company would be at an extreme discount to valuation and normalized EBITDA - I'm talking pennies on the dollar, accounting for their debt. I'm valuing the company at deep EBITDA discounts, mirroring typical PE logic at 3-5x EBITDA, coming to an implied share price of $9.9/share - and even then, I doubt this one would be the first on my list with everything available on the market today.

This is the first company I review that only fulfills one of my criteria - cheapness. I also rate the company a "SELL" here, because I believe the uncertainty from what is going on is too high.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 0 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $9.9/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "SELL" rating as of February 2025.

data.title: Franklin Templeton ClearBridge Small Cap Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: The fourth quarter capped a volatile and demanding year for small cap investors. The Russell 2000 Index returned 2.2% for the quarter and finished the year up 12.8%, trailing the Russell 1000 Index by approximately 450 basis points. A partial rotation away from the dominant AI trade supported value stocks over growth during the quarter, with the Russell 2000 Value Index returning 3.3% compared to a 1.2% gain for the growth index.
  • Contributors: Holdings in Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals (ARWR), Celcuity (CELC) and Photronics (PLAB). Stock selection in the financials and energy sectors and underweight to IT.
  • Detractors: Holdings in Concept Therapeutics (CORT), MP Materials (MP) and Penguin Solutions (PENG). Stock selection in the materials, IT and communication services.
  • Outlook: Despite near-term headwinds, attractive relative valuations and early signs of earnings broadening point to a more constructive setup for patient investors.

Performance Review

  • The Fund generated positive absolute performance but trailed its benchmark in the fourth quarter, as notable strength in financials and energy, along with contributions from select health care holdings, were offset by weakness in materials and consumer discretionary.
  • Beneath the surface, market leadership remained uneven. The quarter began with a continuation of one of the most extreme low-quality rallies on record, as non-earners, low-return on equity companies and high-beta stocks dramatically outperformed, leaving active managers broadly behind. This dynamic briefly gave way to a more constructive environment, as leadership rotated toward value, quality and lower-beta stocks, allowing small caps and active strategies to outperform for a short period. That improvement ultimately proved fleeting, as momentum leadership reasserted itself into year-end, closing the quarter with narrow breadth and elevated speculative activity.
  • Financials strength reflected strong stock selection within consumer finance and specialty credit. OneMain Holdings (OMF), a provider of personal loans and related financial services, benefited from resilient credit trends and improving visibility into earnings as macro conditions stabilized. Encore Capital (ECPG), which focuses on debt recovery and receivables management, also contributed positively as investor sentiment improved toward companies with demonstrated cash flow generation and disciplined balance sheet management in a benign credit environment.
  • Energy was another area of relative strength, driven by company-specific execution and favorable industry fundamentals. Weatherford International (WFRD), an oilfield services provider and a new position, advanced as improved operational performance and sustained upstream activity supported earnings momentum. International Seaways (INSW), a global tanker operator, benefited from constructive tanker market conditions, including favorable freight rates and tighter vessel supply. Green Plains (GPRK), a biofuels and agricultural processing company, contributed as margins improved and investor focus increased on the company's strategic repositioning and longer-term earnings potential.
  • Health care delivered mixed results as a sector but accounted for a large collection of the Strategy's top individual contributors. Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals and Celcuity led performance on continued progress across their respective pipelines, while Axsome Therapeutics (AXSM), Tarsus Pharmaceuticals (TARS) and Avidity Biosciences (RNA) added further positive contributions tied to clinical and regulatory developments.
  • Health care gains were offset by weakness in Concept Therapeutics and Acadia Healthcare (ACHC). Concept declined following the issuance of a Food and Drug Administration Complete Response Letter for its relacorilant program, which introduced uncertainty around the timing and commercial potential of a key pipeline asset. Acadia Healthcare, meanwhile, detracted after lowering its outlook to reflect higher anticipated costs and increased legal-related reserves, which pressured near-term earnings expectations.
  • On the detractor side, materials weighed on relative results due to stock selection. MP Materials, a producer of rare earth materials, declined as sentiment toward certain commodity-exposed names softened following strong prior performance and shifting expectations around demand. Eagle Materials (EXP), a supplier of construction materials, also detracted amid increased sensitivity to cyclical construction trends and input cost dynamics.
  • Consumer discretionary was another area of relative weakness. Stride (LRN), an education services provider, declined as concerns emerged around enrollment trends and near-term growth visibility, while Visteon (VC), an automotive electronics supplier, detracted amid volatility tied to global auto production and demand expectations.
  • We also adjusted our exposure to consumer sectors with the addition of staples company Interparfums (IPAR) and Signet Jewelers (SIG) in discretionary. Interparfums develops and markets prestige fragrances under long-term licensing agreements with global fashion and luxury brands, and we believe the market is undervaluing the company's long-term growth potential as it broadens its portfolio across brands and fragrances. Meanwhile, Signet Jewelers, the largest specialty jewelry retailer in North America, has shown signs of stabilization following a multiyear post-COVID demand contraction. We see improving execution, strong cash generation and ongoing capital returns supporting the company's recovery as demand normalizes.
  • We also exited Alexander & Baldwin (ALEX), a Hawaii-focused real estate company, following the announcement of its agreement to be taken private, and Beta Bionics (BBNX), a medical device company focused on automated insulin delivery systems, as the investment case became increasingly dependent on longer-dated outcomes, leading us to reallocate capital toward opportunities with clearer near-term visibility.

Outlook

  • We remain realistic about the challenges facing active small cap investors in the near term. The market's preference for speculative growth, leverage and momentum has persisted longer than historical precedent would suggest, and periods of narrow leadership can continue to test patience. In such environments, outcomes can be unforgiving, and dispersion can remain elevated.
  • That said, starting conditions matter, and we believe the current setup is increasingly constructive. Small cap valuations remain compelling relative to large caps, earnings leadership has the potential to broaden, and early, albeit intermittent, rotations toward quality and value suggest the market may be becoming incrementally more responsive to fundamentals. While we do not attempt to time inflection points, history suggests that periods of extreme factor dominance are often followed by more balanced leadership.
  • As enthusiasm for low-quality extremes fades and fundamentals reassert themselves, we believe disciplined, bottom-up portfolios focused on durable business models, strong balance sheets and long-term value creation are well-positioned to benefit. While the path forward may not be linear, we are confident that patience, selectivity and adherence to our investment discipline will be rewarded as market conditions normalize.
